findings.quote: Because seeds must be grown out to a fresh crop from time to time before they lose the power to germinate.
findings.note: ARGUABLE: option C states the true general mechanism from para 3 ('every accession slowly loses the power to germinate, and well before it dies it must be grown out ... and banked afresh'); a solver can argue the capacity-matched sections stayed alive precisely because they regenerated in time, which is attractive. But the prompt asks why the two section types DIFFERED, and C is a universal fact true of BOTH sections, so it cannot by itself explain the contrast. Key D ('the length of the wait for regeneration, not the collection's size in itself, that kills an accession') names the differentiator the passage gives ('Each further accession simply waited longer than its seeds could survive') and is clearly best. Beatable, not a double-key.

findings.quote: Count the jars, and the safest vault is the fullest. Open them, and it is not.
findings.note: genre/temperature: aphoristic antithesis and a single-study reversal worked to a symmetrical close read like an essay built to be a test passage; authentic long ELF exemplars (var-2026-verb1-ELF-033 Indie Bookstores, var-2026-verb2-ELF-036 Killer Cats) are multi-source reportage with hard sourced numbers and digressions, not one metaphor hammered. Question style itself is HP-plausible; passage sits at the edge of the exemplar range.

findings.quote: What does Toft's distinction between a 'bankable' current and servicing 'the thing cheaply' imply?
findings.note: Stem leakage: the stem itself names the distinction between 'bankable' and 'cheap to service'. Blind, a reader drops B and C (both say servicing will become cheap, collapsing the stated distinction) and A (addresses only the bankable half), leaving D ('reliable but too expensive to run') as the sole option that honours the distinction -- pickable from the stem alone. PARTIALLY answerable, not a forced structural kill; routed to adjudication (blind pick D).

findings.quote: That the pilot proved the current is bankable, so a utility can be promised a delivery schedule and held to it.
findings.note: ARGUABLE: option A is a near-verbatim restatement of Toft's own words ('We showed the current is bankable - that you can promise a utility a delivery schedule and keep it') and is literally true, which makes it attractive. But the prompt asks what the DISTINCTION between a bankable current and servicing it cheaply implies, and A captures only the 'bankable' pole while ignoring the servicing half that the distinction turns on. Key D ('a tidal resource can be genuinely reliable and predictable while the project built on it stays too expensive to run') is the meaning of the distinction and is clearly best. Beatable, not a double-key.

findings.quote: the tide is free, but the boat is not, and it is the boat that sets the price
findings.note: genre: same manufactured single-pilot reversal skeleton as elf-b6-001, closed on an aphorism; concrete residue thinner than the reportorial exemplars var-2026-verb1-ELF-033 and var-2026-verb2-ELF-036 (invented institutions, no hard sourced figures), the uniform rhythm places it at the edge of range rather than squarely inside it.
